result.markdown:
--- title: "Introduction to GAMs with GAM.jl" author: "Simon Frost" format: html: code-fold: false toc: true gfm: toc: true pdf: toc: true engine: julia --- ## Overview A **Generalized Additive Model (GAM)** extends the linear model by replacing linear predictor terms with smooth functions of covariates. The model takes the form: $$ g(\mu_i) = \beta_0 + f_1(x_{1i}) + f_2(x_{2i}) + \cdots + f_p(x_{pi}) $$ where $g$ is a link function, $\mu_i = \mathbb{E}(y_i | \mathbf{x}_i)$, and each $f_j$ is a smooth function estimated from the data using penalized regression splines. The smooth functions are represented as linear combinations of basis functions: $$ f_j(x) = \sum_{k=1}^{K_j} \beta_{jk} \, b_{jk}(x) $$ Smoothness is controlled by a wiggliness penalty, and the smoothing parameter $\lambda$ is estimated automatically (e.g., by REML). This vignette demonstrates fitting a simple GAM to simulated data using **GAM.jl**. ## Setup ::: {#2 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} using GAM using CSV using StatsAPI: nobs, deviance, predict, coef, residuals, r2 using DataFrames using Plots ``` ::: ## Simulating data We simulate $n = 200$ observations from a sine curve with Gaussian noise: $$ y_i = \sin(2\pi x_i) + \varepsilon_i, \quad \varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.3^2) $$ ::: {#4 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} df = CSV.read("data.csv", DataFrame) x = df.x y = df.y n = nrow(df) first(df, 5) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ```{=html}
5×2 DataFrame
Rowxy
Float64Float64
10.0002388970.361791
20.001380840.322101
30.00157055-0.291095
40.002272970.568826
50.00394834-0.175226
``` ::: ::: ## Fitting a GAM We fit a GAM with a cubic regression spline (`bs=:cr`) smooth of `x` using 15 basis functions: ::: {#6 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} m = gam(@gam_formula(y ~ s(x, k = 15, bs = :cr)), df) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ``` Generalized Additive Model Formula: y ~ 1 Family: Normal Link: IdentityLink Method: REML Parametric coefficients: ─────────────────────────────────────────────────── Coef. Std. Error t Pr(>|t|) ─────────────────────────────────────────────────── (Intercept) -0.100199 0.0204975 -4.89 <1e-05 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────── Approximate significance of smooth terms: ────────────────────────────────────────────────── Smooth edf Ref.df ────────────────────────────────────────────────── s(x,bs=cr) 7.72 14 ────────────────────────────────────────────────── R² (adj) = 0.852 Deviance explained = 85.8% Scale est. = 0.0840 n = 200 ``` ::: ::: The model summary shows parametric coefficients, smooth term EDF, deviance explained, and scale estimate. ### Coefficients and deviance explained ::: {#8 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} println("Number of observations: ", nobs(m)) println("EDF per smooth: ", round.(edf(m); digits = 2)) println("Deviance explained: ", round(r2(m) * 100; digits = 1), "%") println("Scale estimate: ", round(m.scale; digits = 4)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Number of observations: 200 EDF per smooth: [7.72] Deviance explained: 85.8% Scale estimate: 0.084 ``` ::: ::: ## Smooth estimates The `smooth_estimates()` function evaluates the estimated smooth on a regular grid and returns pointwise standard errors: ::: {#10 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} se = smooth_estimates(m; n = 200) x_grid = se.covariates[:x] f_hat = se.estimate f_se = se.se ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ``` 200-element Vector{Float64}: 0.09763142425148784 0.09152560152988946 0.08590579072400169 0.0808676131106106 0.07649627121347419 0.07285701897984644 0.06998441454305089 0.06787288747794756 0.06647185293156631 0.06568775044547812 ⋮ 0.059569766769416506 0.062402813784693076 0.06630289603725327 0.07129730598466999 0.07733529538072287 0.08431009577733312 0.09208297834629607 0.1005022037481864 0.10941501399956634 ``` ::: ::: We plot the smooth estimate with ±2 SE confidence bands, overlaying the true function: ::: {#12 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} plot(x_grid, f_hat; ribbon = 2 .* f_se, fillalpha = 0.25, label = "s(x) ± 2SE", xlabel = "x", ylabel = "f(x)", title = "Estimated smooth with confidence band", linewidth = 2) plot!(x_grid, sin.(2π .* x_grid); label = "true f(x)", linestyle = :dash, linewidth = 2, color = :red) scatter!(df.x, df.y; label = "data", alpha = 0.3, markersize = 2, color = :gray) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ```{=html} ``` ::: ::: ## Prediction at new points We can predict at new covariate values, optionally with standard errors: ::: {#14 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} x_new = DataFrame(x = collect(range(0, 1; length = 50))) μ_hat, se_hat = predict(m, x_new; type = :response, se = true) plot(x_new.x, μ_hat; ribbon = 2 .* se_hat, fillalpha = 0.2, label = "predicted ± 2SE", xlabel = "x", ylabel = "ŷ", title = "Predictions at new data", linewidth = 2) scatter!(df.x, df.y; label = "observed", alpha = 0.3, markersize = 2, color = :gray) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ```{=html} ``` ::: ::: ## Smoothing parameters and REML GAM.jl estimates the smoothing parameter $\lambda$ by minimizing the Restricted Maximum Likelihood (REML) criterion by default. REML is generally preferred over GCV because it: - Avoids the tendency to undersmooth - Provides more stable estimates - Has a natural Bayesian interpretation The fitted model stores the estimated smoothing parameters: ::: {#16 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} println("Estimation method: ", m.method) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Estimation method: REML ``` ::: ::: ## Model diagnostics The `gam_check()` function provides a quick diagnostic summary: ::: {#18 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} gam_check(m) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` GAM checking results ==================== ✓ Model converged Method: REML Scale est. = 0.0840 n = 200 Basis dimension (k) checking results: ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── Smooth k' edf k-index ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── s(x,bs=cr) 14 7.72 0.552 ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── Deviance explained = 85.8% Scale (σ²) = 0.0840 ``` ::: ::: ## Summary In this vignette we: 1. Simulated data from a sine curve with Gaussian noise 2. Fitted a GAM using a cubic regression spline with `k = 15` basis functions 3. Examined the model summary, EDF, and deviance explained 4. Visualized the smooth estimate with confidence bands 5. Predicted at new data points with standard errors 6. Discussed REML-based smoothing parameter estimation The next vignette compares different smooth basis types.
